data.title: U.S.-Iran Ceasefire: Market Turning Point Or Head Fake?
data.description:
A U.S.-Iran ceasefire is prompting investors to reassess market sentiment, geopolitical risk, and the outlook for inflation. What does it mean for positioning from here? Michael Craig, Managing Director, Head of Asset Allocation and Derivatives at TD Asset Management, discusses the latest developments and the implications for Canada.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: A ceasefire between the US and Iran is shifting the tone across global markets. Joining us now to break it all down is Michael Craig, head of asset allocation at TD Asset Management. Michael, thanks for joining us.

Michael Craig: A pleasure.

Greg Bonnell: Right off, what's your view here?

Michael Craig: Yeah, a few things. We're in, obviously, a materially better place today than we were a few days ago. We avoided a humanitarian catastrophe which was being threatened by Trump. We're certainly at a worse place than we were versus a month ago. And I think this truly is, in the spirit of a ceasefire, a timeout, but really with no fundamental issues resolved other than just an agreement that we're going to continue to talk.

But it's going to be challenging to see how things evolve from here in terms of each side walking away with something. Those demands, I think, are going to be quite difficult to work through. So certainly some relief in the markets, but I don't think that you can wave the all-clear signal at this time.

Greg Bonnell: Yeah, you wake up to that relief today or even yesterday evening-- sort of, OK, now we've got this truce. What are we watching now? We've got two weeks, right? What are you watching?

Michael Craig: You certainly look for violations of ceasefire. You want to see hostilities dial back in Lebanon, attacks on Gulf infrastructure, and really, to see what happens with shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. I think the issue today is that Iran now has really seized control of that choke point. And that choke point has tremendous implications for global growth.

And so if the end result is, in the short term, a toll booth, it's probably not the end of the world. I don't think that's an acceptable long-term asset for the Iranians to hold. And this is why I think we come back to this as both sides basically rearm and start to think about what the next phase looks like.

But I think today, this is certainly a better place. We're not going to have oil shortages, which were coming in about -- as I look at my watch, literally in about 10 days. We're facing pretty severe oil shortages in many parts of the world. Always the poorest countries suffer the most, but you would have seen it in Europe and throughout Asia. So that worst-case scenario is, for now, put on the side.

But there's still a lot of work to do. And I think all sides are coming out of this weaker. Iran's been degraded for sure, and the US has certainly burned, I think, a lot of its brand and credibility with allies through this conflict. Allies were not really given a heads-up or had bought into this. And then as it proceeded, they were expected to come and support. After a while, people start to get a bit tired with that kind of approach. And so I think these things are not going to heal anytime soon.

Greg Bonnell: So we'll take this moment of relief. Of course, there's important things to watch, as you laid out. What are the implications for the markets for geopolitics?

Michael Craig: Yeah, so I'm going to start short term and then long term. The short term is not nearly as interesting, but I think we need to talk about it because there's been a pretty euphoric move today. Look, positioning going into this last month was brutal for hedge fund-type investors, real money investors. Tough month, but really, really bad for the leveraged community.

And so going into last night, I think positioning was quite clean. And so that's why you have-- as the tide comes back in, everyone's rushing in. And so you're seeing in North American markets reasonable moves, 2%, 3%. But you start looking at other markets -- emerging is up 6%. Korea is up 10%. Europe is up 5%. European bonds are rallying 20 basis points. Big moves. And I think that just is more of a testament to positioning had been cleaned out. I wouldn't look at the markets and say, oh, the markets are rallying. Everything must be good. This is really a technical rally, in my opinion.

Longer term, it's complicated. If you step back, the key geopolitical rivalry remains the US and the Chinese. And neither side wants to go into that without allies. They need allies because they're so big that they can't go at it alone. And what you have here -- well, in many ways, Europe has probably moved somewhat more in between those two orbits, totally irritated by this conflict. Certainly, the Global South will look at this and say, how much trust can we put into the US?

China comes out looking OK because they just basically played a role as a bit of a sideshow to this and didn't really get involved, which is typically -- the Chinese are very much focused on their interests and what they're trying to accomplish and not trying to solve problems in other parts of the world. And for Canada, we've been bullish Canada. I would say, having through this, I'm more bullish now because it's just more pressure on our country to deliver what the world needs.

And it's not just getting commodities. It's getting commodities from trustworthy suppliers -- and not just commodities. It really starts to -- we should be thinking about refining our goods. We should think about value-added processing. Because when you look at the globe, it's a pretty attractive place to be sourcing that thing. And this is not a one-month or three-month view.

This is a view, I think, that, if we're right, will persist through the remainder of my investment career, which hopefully is a long time, as it takes a long time for these things to build out. But I think that we come out of this looking -- oh my gosh, we have this opportunity, this phenomenal opportunity, to be a force for good in the world and a consistent supply of energy and resources, critical minerals, et cetera. So from that perspective, I see a tremendous opportunity. It's just whether we can capitalize on it. And that is always where the risk lies.

Greg Bonnell: In terms of asset allocation in the longer term-- because in the short term, we're worried about the soaring price of crude, what it could mean for inflation, what it could mean for interest rates, all very in-the-moment kind of things. I mean, we still have to worry about those things or think about those things. But when you think about capital allocation with what's happening and where we're headed, what are your thoughts?

Michael Craig: OK, so I can't say that we had any-- if you asked me three months ago, would there be a war in Iran, I would have probably said no. But if you asked me three months ago, are we going to have a challenge in the Middle East for the next five years, I'd say, likely.

Our view with capital allocation is that the world is far more complex today than it has been in our time, that the result of that is going to lead to much more volatile inflation and growth. So much of our career, we've had this declining or disinflationary backdrop and growth coming, ebbing and flowing. Now I think growth is more volatile, and inflation is more volatile.

And that gets you to looking at portfolios not just in terms of bonds and equities, which a traditional balanced fund is, but is adding elements that perform in periods of excessive growth or excessive inflation. And that gets to a much more complex multi-asset portfolio. But the result of that is a return profile that is more comfortable for investors.

And ultimately, the most important thing for us to do is to ensure that our investors know that we have their back and stay invested because when you sell in a moment of panic, and then nine months later, you decide to reinvest, generally, those periods are when you see the highest returns in the market. And that is very hard. Put another way, that is like adding years of work to your life to make up for not being invested in that short a period of time because that's when you see these big returns.

So for us, it's allocating to commodities, allocating to certain private assets that are creating cash flow. So I don't advocate speculating on the next big thing. It's too complicated. And you need a lot of those bets because most of them go to zero. But actually, looking at private investments that are creating cash flows today, looking at other areas within the market where we can grab return that is uncorrelated to bonds and equities. And so that is where the efforts are.

And when you have that approach, things like this occur, but you're prepared for it. Well, last month, stocks and bonds fell off, but we had other areas that worked. Commodities this year are up. Even after today's selloff in commodities, they're still up 20-plus percent this year. And that is the goal of it, is to make sure that you never want everything-- you never want all of your investments working at the same time, and you certainly don't want them all not working at the same time. And that's the whole power of diversification.

Original Post

data.title: March CPI: Energy-Led Inflation Jump Complicates Fed Outlook
data.description:
The March CPI report arrives at a complicated moment for the inflation narrative, as an external shock tied to the Middle East conflict interrupts what had been a gradual cooling in price pressures. Headline inflation moved sharply higher on the back of energy, but the underlying details paint a more nuanced picture, with core measures showing less momentum than expected and several key segments remaining relatively contained. That mix of a clear upside surprise in the headline alongside softer underlying trends leaves both markets and policymakers in a difficult position, trying to determine whether this marks the start of a renewed inflation cycle or just a temporary disruption layered on top of an otherwise moderating trend.

CPI

U.S. consumer prices surged in March, as expected, as a flare-up in energy costs related to the conflict in the Middle East caused headline inflation to accelerate. The CPI increased 0.9% MoM in the final month of Q1 2026, the largest single-month rise since June 2022, pushing the annual rate to 3.3% YoY, a nearly two-year high. However, the details of the report do suggest that inflation outside of energy saw more of a tepid rise. Specifically, CPI ex energy was up 0.2% MoM and 2.6% YoY, only slightly higher than February’s reading of 2.5% YoY.

The volatile components in today’s CPI release moved quite differently in March, but of course, the strength in energy more than offset the food segment.

  • The energy index jumped 10.9% MoM in March, the largest MoM increase since September 2005. The gasoline index actually posted its largest MoM increase ever, up 21.2% MoM. Energy went from contributing 3.2 bps to annual CPI inflation in February to 81.6 bps. The volatility, of course, was due to energy goods prices driven by surges in energy commodity prices. Energy services saw a smaller 0.4% MoM rise and was a non-factor more or less.
  • In contrast to energy, the food index posted its weakest print since April 2025 of 0.0% MoM in March. The food at home index fell -0.2% MoM, also the lowest since April 2025, as four out of six of the grocery indexes declined. Specifically, the two largest food subgroups deflated, with cereals & bakery products (-0.6% MoM) and meat, fish, and eggs (-0.6% MoM) recording solid declines. Food inflation eased to its lowest since November 2025, dropping to 2.7% YoY.

The headlines will understandably focus on the surge in energy prices, but the softness in food is just as notable when looking through the composition of the report. The divergence between the two categories was stark, with energy driving the bulk of the upside in headline inflation while food showed little sign of pass-through from those same cost pressures, raising the possibility that higher gasoline prices may already be weighing on discretionary spending elsewhere, including at the grocery store, though it is still early to draw firm conclusions from a single month of data.

From a policy perspective, both categories tend to carry less weight in shaping the Fed’s reaction function given their volatility, and the energy spike in particular is likely to be viewed as transitory unless it begins to feed more persistently into broader price measures, a dynamic that will depend heavily on how the situation in the Middle East evolves and whether any supply disruptions become more entrenched.

Core CPI

While headline inflation perked up, core CPI inflation was not as active, up 0.2% MoM and 2.6% YoY in March. Both of these rates were actually below analyst expectations of increases of 0.3% MoM and 2.7% YoY. Indeed, the annual rate did see an acceleration of about 14 bps from February to March, but this was actually a slower acceleration than expected. Both core goods and core services played roles in this, as they both posted relatively cool MoM rates in March.

  • Core goods prices were up just 0.1% MoM in March, matching the February pace. Several segments saw declines, keeping a lid on goods price inflation: household furnishings (-0.2% MoM), transportation goods (-0.1% MoM), and medical care goods (-1.0% MoM). The heavy-weight auto indexes both contributed to the cool print, with new vehicle prices up just 0.1% MoM and used vehicle prices down -0.4% MoM. Outside of a 1.0% MoM increase in apparel, there were plenty of signals that the flare-up in goods inflation was abating as tariff pass-through effects dwindle.
  • Core services prices saw the smallest rise since September 2025, up just 0.2% MoM in March. The main driver of weakness was a flat MoM print in medical care services, while a tame 0.3% MoM in the shelter index was reported as well. There were some pockets of inflationary pressure within transport services (+0.6% MoM), like auto maintenance & repair up 1.3% MoM and airfares up 2.7% MoM (now up 14.9% YoY), but declines in recreation services (-0.4% MoM) and other personal services (-0.8% MoM) balanced those out.

While the core segments were not directly exposed to the conflict in the Middle East, there are some signs that higher energy costs and related disruptions may have filtered through. The most obvious example remains airfares, which posted another firm increase as airlines adjusted pricing in response to higher jet fuel costs. More broadly, transport-related services also picked up, with moving, storage, and freight rising 1.9% MoM and motor vehicle maintenance and repair up 1.3% MoM, suggesting some near-term pass-through from higher operating and logistics costs.

There were also a few firmer prints on the goods side, including apparel (+1.0% MoM) and other recreational goods (+1.8% MoM), which may reflect tighter supply chain conditions and higher shipping costs, though the signal here is less clear and could prove temporary if energy prices stabilize.

Given the circumstances, it is somewhat difficult to cleanly assess the underlying trend across the core segments in March. On the surface, the softer-than-expected core CPI prints on both a MoM and YoY basis do offer some relief, and may suggest that analysts initially overstated the degree of near-term indirect impacts from the conflict into core segments. However, there were still clear pockets where those effects began to emerge, and with no complete resolution to the conflict in place through the first 10 days of April, there is a growing risk that these cost pressures broaden out as higher input and transportation costs are passed through to consumers. That dynamic would be problematic for the inflation outlook, which was already showing signs of stickiness amid elevated wage growth and the easing in financial conditions following recent rate cuts.

Market Reaction

We return back to the reaction in financial markets to get a sense of how investors are feeling about inflation once again. This March CPI report has left more of a mark because it includes the impact of the Middle East conflict, the main driver of market sentiment these days. In general, we are seeing a hawkish reaction to the hot headline CPI inflation rate, apparently ignoring the core CPI inflation miss.

Equity futures moved sideways in the two hours after the CPI report was released as traders fought to maintain the positive market sentiment from the 2-week ceasefire established earlier this week. The S&P 500 (SP500), NASDAQ (COMP:IND), and Russell 2000 (RTY) were all higher on the day, all up around 0.2-0.5%, suggesting there was a light risk-on sentiment this morning.

Once again, we see a clearer reaction in the bond market where yields jumped as soon as the report was released. However, the rise in yields, around +3 bps in the 2-year yield (US2Y) and +2 bps in the 10-year yield (US10Y), was only really enough to counter the drop in Thursday’s trading period. The broad message from financial markets seems to be that there is an increasing feeling of uneasiness around the inflation trend, which threatens to keep interest rates from falling.

Most of the concern now stems from the fact that this CPI report is likely to reinforce the FOMC’s view that inflation risks are skewed to the upside through the remainder of 2026. Market pricing shifted in that direction immediately following the release, with Fed funds futures moving to reflect a very high probability (~70%) that rates remain unchanged for the rest of the year. In many ways, the Fed is being forced into a holding pattern, as the re-emergence of energy-driven inflation tied to the Middle East conflict comes just as earlier tariff-related pressures had begun to fade.

As a result, policymakers have little room to ease, particularly if these new cost pressures begin to broaden out. That backdrop becomes even more restrictive when paired with a labor market that continues to show resilience, as recent data suggest, leaving rate expectations elevated and reinforcing a higher-for-longer stance in policy.

data.title: It's A Bit Premature To Downgrade Preferred Bank
data.description:
Since the start of this year, things have not gone exceptionally well for shareholders of Preferred Bank (PFBC). Although profitability increased for the business in its most recent quarter, the share price has pulled back 7.7% since I reaffirmed it as a "Buy" candidate in early January. By comparison, the S&P 500 is down 6.2% over that same period. The good news is that, since I originally turned bullish on the company in July of 2023, the stock has risen 49.7%. That is above the 41.5% jump that the market enjoyed. So at least, over the long haul, my call on it is paying off.

The most recent data available does show some signs of weakness, especially when it comes to net interest margin. Having said that, asset quality remains robust, and the stock price looks cheap relative to earnings. I will say that it's not as attractive as it used to be. However, I don't yet see a reason why I would downgrade it to a "Hold" at this time. Instead, reaffirming it as a soft "Buy," even though the market is currently moving against me on it, seems to make the most sense.

Preferred Bank isn’t prime for a downgrade

As I mentioned at the start of this article, one of the downsides to Preferred Bank as of late has been its net interest margin. In the chart above, you can see that for the final quarter of 2025, this metric was 3.74%. That's down from the 4.06% reading that we got a year earlier. With many banks that I have looked at lately, we have actually seen the opposite transpire. This is because, even though you would think that declining interest rates might be harmful for banks, they can actually prove beneficial in the short run if they are structured appropriately. Interest-bearing deposits are overwhelmingly floating rate obligations. So as interest rates drop, financial institutions that have deposits can slash how much they are paying depositors. By comparison, many assets adjust lower more slowly, especially because of interest rate floors and some assets being fixed.

As the chart above illustrates, however, even though the company did benefit from a decline in the cost of interest-bearing deposits, the returns that it has been receiving on loans have dropped quite a bit. They declined from 8.01% to 7.32% year over year. The fact of the matter is that 55% of the company's loans carry interest rates that adjust with changes in the prime rate. Another 25% involves interest rates tied to the SOFR and other indices, all of which reprice periodically. These can be described as adjustable-rate loans. While the company does have interest rate floors on 97% of floating rate loans and on 45% of adjustable rate loans, that doesn't mean that the rates won't fall as interest rates are cut. So naturally, with only 20% of loans fixed or tied to a CD, it's not shocking that a deterioration in interest rates would have a negative impact on loan returns.

In the chart above, you can see that this caused a reduction in net interest income for the final quarter of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. This was not the only contributor. We also saw a $2.3 million jump in its provision for credit losses year over year. But still, that proved to be a negative for the institution. The good news is that non-interest income jumped from $3.6 million to $8.1 million. But this was largely because of a $3.6 million gain on the sale of other real estate owned by the business. That should not be viewed as a permanent change. Still, that was enough to help push up net profits from $30.2 million to $34.8 million. And for the year as a whole, the $133.6 million in net income generated by the company exceeded the $130.7 million reported the previous year. If we do strip out this gain that the company booked, we would get a small increase in profitability to $131.1 million for 2025.

We also should be looking at certain parts of the balance sheet for the company as well. In the chart above, you can see bonds, deposits, securities, cash, and debt. The most important of these, in my view, would be deposits. Those went from $5.92 billion in 2024 to $6.35 billion in 2025. That's a pretty nice increase. But it does require context. For starters, 42.1% of the company’s deposits happen to be brokered. This means that they are high-cost deposits borrowed from other financial institutions. It's also worth noting that 50% of all deposits were classified as uninsured. This does increase the risk profile of the company. I especially don't like the uninsured deposit exposure. However, management helps to offset some of this risk by keeping debt in check. It was only $348.7 million in the most recent quarter. That was up from what the company saw in 2024. But it's still modest in the grand scheme of things.

If we were to use the adjusted net profits that I calculated for 2025 as an example, we can see in the chart above that shares of Preferred Bank are trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 8.2. That is comfortably below the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. It also puts the company below all but one of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart. Unfortunately, I cannot say that it's cheap relative to book value and tangible book value as well. In the chart below, you can see that four of the five companies I stacked it up against ended up being cheaper than it was when it came to both of these metrics.

Normally, this would justify more of a neutral stance than it does a bullish one. However, asset quality for the company is impressive. The firm ended the 2025 fiscal year with a return on assets reading of 1.82%. That is significantly above the 1% minimum that I typically look for. And it also is above any of the five institutions that I compared it to in the first chart below. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see a return on equity reading of 17.59%. That is also meaningfully higher than the 10% minimum that I look for, and it puts the company above other comparable enterprises by an attractive margin.

Perhaps the only area where I am a bit more reserved would be when it comes to the company’s credit quality. In general, I like to see that no more than 0.50% of both loans and assets are classified as non-performing. But for loans, that number was 0.85% at the end of the year, while for assets it was 0.72%. This is certainly something that should be examined more closely, especially as broader economic conditions become more challenged.

Takeaway

Based on the data available right now, I would say that Preferred Bank is interesting, but it's no longer the easy money that it once was. I won't go so far as to say that downgrading the stock is necessary at this time. But I do think that it deserves only the softest of "Buy" ratings in light of certain changes that we have seen. The decline in net interest margin is particularly disappointing. And if we do see further drops, a downgrade would be warranted.

data.title: Private Credit Is Becoming A Quagmire
data.description:
A ceasefire continues to hold for now in the Middle East, even if the flow through the Strait of Hormuz is still a trickle of what it was before hostilities commenced late in February. Not surprisingly, oil has sold off after a huge move up in March and early April. For now, the headlines from the region are not nearly as negative or frequent as they were to start the week when the administration was threatening to blow Iran back to the 'stone age.'

If the Iranian conflict continues to fade from the front pages of the financial media, the deterioration in the private credit market is there to take its place as one of the biggest worries for investors. Since problems first popped up at Blue Owl Capital (OWL) in the back half of February, issues have accelerated. And this week has been no exception, with several snippets of new bad news for this niche of the credit markets that is rapidly becoming a quagmire.

The private credit market saw many large funds by the likes of Apollo Global Management (APO), Morgan Stanley (MS), Blackstone (BX), Cliffwater, Ares Management Corporation (ARES), and BlackRock (BLK) 'gate' redemptions in March, which increased the panic level throughout this space. Of note, redemption requests have been rising for a few quarters now.

And problems were becoming more apparent in the private sector before these latest redemption requests. At the end of 2025, Fitch Ratings pegged the default rate in the private credit sector at a record 9.2%. Of note, this is more than 60% of the way to UBS's recent worst-case scenario of a 15% default rate in private credit.

It also should be noted that private credit funds' over nine percent default rate doesn't account for the increasing utilization of Payment in Kind, or PIK, by these lenders. This keeps the loan 'current' even if it doesn't involve a scheduled loan payment and just tacks more interest to the back of the loan term.

Then on March 11th, JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) fired a shot across the bow of the entire private credit sector when it marked down the value of some loans to private credit funds and reduced their borrowing capacity. This followed a review by the bank and primarily impacted loans to the software sector. US banks have approximately $300 billion in loans to private credit, and other banks have not publicly gone the JP Morgan route yet around cutting lines to private credit funds.

This week, in a classic closing the barn door after most of the horses have escaped fashion, Moody's downgraded the flagship fund of Blue Owl Capital Inc. to negative due largely to huge investor redemption requests. Moody's had recently also taken a $14 billion private credit fund run by KKR & Co. (KKR) and Future Standard to Junk status as well.

The private credit space, at a size of roughly $1.8 trillion, pales in size to the approximate $70 trillion value of the U.S. equity market. Prudent investors need to keep a close eye on the continued deterioration of conditions around private credit. Moody's had projected the private credit market with $2 trillion in AUM, predicted to grow to $4 trillion by 2030 at the start of 2026.

Following new regulations following the Great Financial Crisis, it became more problematic for banks to hold riskier loans. A huge chunk of previous banks' mortgage volume has transitioned to government-sponsored entities like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, as well as the VA and FHA.

Private credit has stepped into the breach and has filled demand from the corporate mid-market sector. Unfortunately, a big chunk of these loans has gone to the software industry, where concerns that these businesses will see significant disruption from AI are growing. Some $330 billion of this high-yield, leveraged debt to software and technology companies will come due by 2028, according to a report from Bloomberg this week.

And the Wall Street Journal recently audited four large private credit funds and found their average exposure to the software sector was higher than these funds had stated in their filings (above). There are numerous potential ramifications to the economy if investor redemptions and default rates within private credit funds continue to rise. This would mean less funding available to mid-market firms resulted via some sort of 'credit crunch' for this part of the economy.

Private credit and private equity also have a somewhat incestuous relationship, as private credit lends to private equity companies and has become the dominant form of financing leveraged buyouts, or LBOs.

And unfortunately, the growing problems in this space are not the only credit area that is growing increasingly problematic. Delinquency rates for student loans, auto obligations, and credit card debt continue to grow. These categories represent roughly $4.5 trillion of combined consumer debt.

Investors also have the struggling commercial real estate, or CRE, market to worry about, where some $5 trillion in debt is outstanding. Nearly $900 billion of which will need to be refinanced in 2026. The March CMBS (Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities) delinquency rate moved up again, by 41 bps to 7.55%. And this does not include 'extend & pretend' actions. If loans past their maturity date but current on interest payments, which are classified as performing matured balloon, were included in these numbers, the overall CMBS delinquency rate would be just over 9%. The overall CMBS delinquency rate peaked during the Great Financial Crisis, peaking at 10.34% in July 2012 by way of comparison.

Prior to the Covid Pandemic, the CRE CMBS delinquency rate was just under two percent, it should be noted. Investors should pay particular attention to Office and Multi-Family sectors, which account for roughly 70% of CRE debt outstanding. Multi-Family's CMBS delinquency rates have moved through 7%. Office properties have nearly 12% delinquency rates, significantly above that of the peak of the Great Financial Crisis.

In summary, private credit is becoming a larger quagmire by the week. While the market is small compared to the overall equity market, there could be negative impacts on adjacent sectors. In addition, and importantly, private credit is not the only part of the credit markets that is consistently deteriorating right now. And this trend is one to watch for prudent investors, and in my opinion, it is not priced appropriately within overall market valuations.

data.title: XT: More Sensitive Than General Market To Cost Of Capital Changes
data.description:
The iShares Future Exponential Technologies ETF (XT) is an international, but still mostly US, tech-focused ETF that tries to capture a disruptor factor in markets. It is quite correlated to the US markets in general.

The main thing we want to discuss is the reinflation factor around the geopolitical developments of the Iran War. High multiple ETFs, unsurprisingly like tech-focused XT, are levered to cost of capital assumptions, especially in the longer term.

XT, which has a 30x P/E compared to the S&P 500's (IVV) 27x P/E, had fallen more sharply and rebounded more sharply in response to the latest geopolitical developments, namely what seemed to be a ceasefire, though now again in question. This reflects the association of a higher PE, meaning more value in the horizon, to cost of capital assumptions that bear harder on companies with implied cash flows that are more distantly deferred.

We continue to be worried about the yield curve and cost of capital. Dim hopes for the ceasefire, low US jobless applications, and inevitable CPI spikes that could interact with inflation expectations all threaten high multiple market plays, which are constituted by any ETF heavily indexed towards tech like XT. With the inflation aspect of the case being a factor across the globe due to the higher oil price, the XT's geographic diversity relative to the IVV isn't enough to mitigate the danger.

XT Breakdown

XT is actually less exposed to the mega-cap exposures in the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV). There is less exposure to NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), about half the exposure at a little under 4%, and the trillion-cap tech stocks don't dominate the top 10 holdings as much. Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) instead holds the top spot at a little over 4% exposure. So XT is certainly not value weighted, and the slightly more involved balancing does mean a higher expense ratio than the IVV at 0.46% compared to 0.03%.

While IVV is all US, XT has 68% US exposure and a little bit of China too at 4%, as well as European stocks at above 10% exposure, with Japan and Korea a little under 10%. Nonetheless, despite less exposure to the US market, which is one of the highest multiple markets in the world, the exceptional disruptor and tech focus of the XT puts it at 30x PE compared to IVV's 27x.

IT is 40% of the ETF. Healthcare is 26% with more risky pharma, then 18% for industrials and consumer discretionary in total. The healthcare exposure at 26% is considerably more than the 9.25% in the IVV. This lessens specific exposures to AI in the XT as compared to the IVV, but it is likely still heavily correlated to the AI trading factor, as its IT exposure is higher at almost 40% compared to 33% of the IVV. Healthcare is still a high multiple part of the market, so all the comments around sensitivity to capital costs still apply.

The consequence of this is that with higher earnings growth expectations, more of the market's implied underlying cash flows are in the future instead of the present, meaning more periods over which to compound a discount factor. Therefore, the relevant cost of capital assumptions up to that longer horizon have more of a compounded effect and therefore affect valuations more harshly when they change. The yield curve has clearly shifted up across the board, but the delta between the current curve and where it was a month ago has lessened, perhaps due to the initial ceasefire announcement and the associated downward movement in oil prices, which are the primary factors for the world's reinflation. This helps explain the overall market rebound the day before yesterday when the cost of capital assumptions declined, driven by changes in the risk-free rates.

Bull Case

  • Assuming the ceasefire actually holds, which may depend on no further hostilities on the Lebanon and Hezbollah front as well as for Israel, the immediate and acute energy crisis would be lessened. Regardless, some traffic was also making it through Hormuz by shipping risk-takers, according to on-the-ground accounts. The oil futures curve has shown expectations for an earlier normalisation. All this has reduced risk-free rate assumptions again and has helped lessen the discounting of the horizon values.

Bear Case

This is the case that convinces us more and has more cumulative elements both in the short and medium term:

  • The market is sensitive to signs of overbuilding by the data center builders, which could mean the valuable hardware stocks like Nvidia have had their growth overly extrapolated. We are worried about DeepSeek risks for the market, where architectural improvement could cliff hardware demand for training AI suddenly. XT contains some of these exposures, though possibly less than the IVV, also because of the XT healthcare skew. But the higher overall IT exposure still puts it in harm's way, most likely from this risk.
  • In the shorter term, we aren't convinced that the energy crisis is over. On one hand, the ceasefire is very fragile, and it doesn't help that Iran is making ambitious demands, including reparations and continued control of the Strait of Hormuz. Other than the demands possibly tanking the ceasefire deal, if it goes through, we'd expect high tolls on passing traffic that put a high floor on oil prices. The oil futures curve may have gotten ahead of itself in assuming a mid-year normalization.
  • Furthermore, jobless claims weren't that high, which is underlying inflation. Additionally, a CPI spike is fully expected, as much as up to 0.9% in one month, because of the higher oil prices, which could start reflecting in consumer inflation expectations once quoted. Expectations have already started to climb as of the April 7th surveys by the NY Fed, 3.4% up from 3% last month in annual expectations. At the same time, consumption was iffy, which isn't good for any part of the market, but especially for the industrial and consumer discretionary exposures at close to 20% of XT. There are a lot of leveraged purchase markets like automotive in the ETF; Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is at 3.4%.

Bottom Line

We think there are more risks than supports currently to the general market, especially as it concerns cost of capital assumptions, which depend on a geopolitical resolution to lessen discounting effects. XT is a more amplified and sensitive version of the cost of capital, reflected in the more dramatic swings YTD. Not being able to predict one way or another what will happen with this Iran war, we are most comfortable avoiding these high multiple ETFs altogether, which are sensitive to the associated yield curve considerations.

data.title: Corning: Great Company Amid AI Boom, But Don't Value It Like A Hot Tech Stock
data.description:
It’s easy to admire Corning (GLW). The company’s prowess in glass, ceramics, and other fields has spurred innovation and provided great value for companies and end users. Growth has and likely will remain strong going forward. Profitability is solid too, especially for a mature industrial company. Alas, GLW is still not a hot-growth, high-margin tech stock, but as I’ll dig into later, it’s still not a high-margin, hot-growth tech stock, yet its valuation metrics treat it that way.

Corning’s share prices have soared over the last year, and growth accelerated in late January 2026 after an AI data center deal with Meta (META) emerged. Ultimately, I believe that GLW currently faces many of the same downsides and risks stemming from a potential AI bubble while also offering lower upside. Even if you believe the Artificial Intelligence boom is the real deal (technologically speaking, I believe in it), there are better AI opportunities elsewhere, in my humble opinion.

Corning’s AI Tailwinds Are Tempting But Not Substantial Enough

Corning is one of the more recent of a long line of companies to enjoy strong AI tailwinds. If AI truly is a bubble, it’s one of the largest and most widespread in history. At this point, I think it could eclipse the dot-com bubble because it has spread so far and wide outside of the tech sector. Construction, various commodities, electricity generation, and government spending are all being driven rather directly by the AI boom. Many, if not most, industries have attracted considerable AI investments, although exposure varies widely. In some cases, AI companies themselves are shouldering risks, such as Anthropic moving into healthcare. In other cases, AI is creating opportunities outside of the tech industry, such as aiding with drug discovery or creating digital twins to then drive industrial development.

The broad, horizontal impact of AI development will create lots of opportunities for entrepreneurs, tech companies, and investors. When Meta announced a major $6 billion deal with Corning, share prices soared. If you happened to have held GLW before the announcement, good for you and congrats on the big gains. For a company that produces “just” ~$15 billion per year in revenue, a $6 billion deal that will run through 2030 with Meta to supply fiber optic cables certainly is a big deal. Yet at the same time, $6 billion over the course of several years isn’t really that big of a deal for a company with a market cap topping $125 billion.

Corning is going to need to expand production capacities in North Carolina to fulfill the Meta contract. This expansion is expected to add ~130 jobs, suggesting it's rather small in scope. The company also announced that it is adding production capacity in Poland to meet AI demand by building its third manufacturing facility in the country. Given that 2,500 jobs are expected to be created, this suggests a rather extensive investment.

Keep in mind that these investments will increase risks. The risks in this case will now extend beyond the general AI bubble risks and will also include the risks generated by Meta itself. While Meta is spending lavishly on AI, worries over high CapEx have drawn skepticism, the company recently delayed publicly releasing an AI model due to poor performance, and thus far, Meta has lagged behind industry leaders, such as Google, Anthropic, and OpenAI. If Meta stumbles, that big, shiny contract with Corning could evolve into an albatross around Corning’s neck.

Of course, Corning isn't resting on its laurels with the META deal. The company is executing a broader "Project Springboard" to add $4 billion in annual sales by the end of 2026 and to secure a 20% operating margin by the end of the year. Analysts have been upgrading GLW, sending share prices higher in recent days. More deals with other AI and data center companies could and likely will emerge. Yet each deal will expose Corning to risks in a highly competitive, speculative industry. If an AI company that partners with Corning folds or falls behind and exits the AI race, GLW will come under pressure. Risks are a fact of existence for companies, but at current valuations, investors are being asked to shoulder risks on par with (or at least approaching) a potential high-margin AI cash bull, but really, GLW is more of a mid-margin cash cow.

I’ll dig into this more in the next section, but Corning’s margins simply don’t approach what you’d expect for a hot tech company. Yet its valuation metrics are now on par with and even exceeding high-margin, high-growth tech companies. AI data center deals will drive revenues and should help Corning sustain high profit margins for a more traditional industrial firm. However, that likely won’t translate into the types of profits and free cash flows you’d expect to see with its valuation.

GLW Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

I normally like to take a considerable look at a company’s recent performance in terms of fundamentals, like revenue growth, etc. However, since Corning is in a pivotal change stage following the Meta deal and the prospects of future deals, I don’t believe past performance, including recent performance, will tell us much. As such, I adopt a more forward-looking approach centered around a reverse DCF calculation as outlined later in this section.

We should keep in mind that the mega deal offered with Meta will not be reflected in much of the data currently reported for GLW. The company will release its next quarterly report at the end of the month, but that too won’t capture everything from the new deal. Regardless, GLW’s current TTM GAAP PE ratio of ~80 is extremely high. Even if earnings double and that PE ratio is cut in half, it’d still be sky-high at ~40.

Perhaps the most important metric going forward will be the net income margin. Currently, GLW’s 11.15% profit margin is quite healthy for a mature industrial company but falls well short of the +25% net income margins enjoyed by Google (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta, etc., as shown above. Likewise, the gross margin is quite healthy at ~35%, but well behind what we see with many hot tech stocks, as shown below. Can Corning improve its margins with mega deals like the one signed with Meta? Probably. Will it be enough to justify such high PE and PS ratios? I doubt it.

To further explore the type of performance GLW would need to secure to justify its current stock price, I ran a simple reverse DCF calculation with a 5-year growth period. Given how rapid technologies and markets are changing, I’m increasingly wary of projecting beyond 5 years. Quite simply, the world is going to look quite different in 5 years. I used a 3% terminal growth rate to match general historical economic growth rates and set the share price to GLW’s most recent post-market reading of $156.3 (as of submitting this article). I use a standardized 10% discount rate, as I believe that’s a reasonable hurdle rate for investors to pursue. I also use levered free cash flows to create an equity model.

The results, as we see above, suggest an implied levered FCF growth of 82%. While the deal with Meta and potentially other hyperscalers likely will spur rapid free cash flow growth, +80% sustained over 5 years is well beyond a tall task. Even if we adjusted the model to project out to 10 years, the FCF growth would remain above 30%, and sustaining such high growth over such a long period of time would be immensely difficult.

Further, I’d expect margins to come under pressure as more deals are announced. If nothing else, the capital expenditure needed to meet demand may put pressure on the margins. Corning will already have to expand production just to meet the Meta contract, and will likely need to further expand as more large contracts roll in. I also suspect that the market, top-to-bottom, will simply grow more competitive as other companies look to cash in on the AI boom.

GLW does offer a dividend, with the FWD yield reported at .76%. This is nice but not nearly enough to change the calculus. Dividends may improve in the future, but I wouldn't count on this as an income stock. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools assign a hold rating, as illustrated below, with valuation getting an F grade.

I don’t enjoy betting against firms that I generally like and find impressive from a business standpoint. I think Corning is an innovative company and will remain a profitable and strong business. However, the valuation here has grown too high, and the risks are simply too great. As an investment, I have to pass, and the very high valuation results from the reverse DCF, along with the high P/E and P/S ratios, lead to a sell rating.

Earnings Preview

GLW will release its next earnings on the 28th of April, before markets open. While I have assigned a sell rating, this rating is based more on long-term prospects. An earnings beat here and a stock pop would not be unexpected, as GLW has consistently beaten estimates in recent quarters (illustrated below). EPS is expected to shrink from the previous quarter but be up by more than 27% YOY. In the latter half of 2026, even stronger performance is expected. If GLW falls short (I find that unlikely) of expectations, a severe stock drop could unfold as longer-term views sour.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Corning has proven to be an innovative company able to adapt and adjust with the times. They are not the type of company I like to bet against, and perhaps they’ll prove me wrong. The best prospects will likely come from an accelerating AI boom. If the expansion can continue to build momentum, rising demand will make it easier for GLW to sustain and even expand margins, thus growing free cash flows.

If another mega deal with another client, say Nvidia (NVDA), is announced, stock prices will likely soar. Nvidia already ranks among Corning's clients and is actively involved in the next generation of AI data center hardware. More deals would not be surprising. Even in such cases, I wouldn't be surprised if I reevaluated GLW and came away with another sell rating. However, investors already holding GLW could pocket a nice profit even if I'm right in the long term and the company is overvalued.

Corning may also unveil new technologies/products that will create new, highly profitable revenue streams. The company’s Gorilla Glass lineup has been one of the big winners with the proliferation of smartphones. Perhaps Corning has “tricks” up its sleeve and unveils hot new products. Ultimately, Corning has managed to keep up with the times and evolving technologies, generally maintaining a competitive, high-value-added edge while other glass and chemical companies have been pressured by commoditization. The company could simply outdo my expectations.

Key Takeaway: I Wish I Had Bought Months Ago, But The Valuation Is Too High Now

Corning as a business should continue to do well, and I suspect bright days are ahead, especially if the AI boom can sustain momentum. GLW as an investment is simply too rich for my recommendation right now. Investors are right to be excited about the Meta mega deal, but it’s still not enough for me to justify an 80+ P/E ratio or such a high PS ratio. The current share price’s implied levered FCF growth is likely beyond reach even with immense AI tailwinds.

Meanwhile, if the AI boom slows or Meta’s ambitions stumble, GLW could be exposed to risks comparable to a hot AI-tech stock. Yet I don’t believe it can offer comparable upside to such stocks. As a result, I assign a sell rating to GLW. If the valuation cools off in the future, I'd gladly take another look at GLW as an investment opportunity.
